name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
summary: PT-141 (bremelanotide) is a synthetic cyclic heptapeptide melanocortin receptor agonist studied for sexual desire and arousal. Unlike PDE5 inhibitors that act on blood flow, it works centrally in the brain; the acetate form (Vyleesi) is FDA-approved for hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in premenopausal women.
mechanism:
Bremelanotide is a cyclic seven-amino-acid peptide (stabilized by a lactam bridge between Asp and Lys residues) derived from the alpha-MSH / Melanotan-II lineage but re-engineered for receptor selectivity and without the tanning-focused activity of Melanotan II. It non-selectively activates melanocortin receptors with an approximate order of potency MC1R > MC4R > MC3R > MC5R > MC2R; at therapeutic doses the effects most relevant to sexual function are mediated by MC4R (with MC1R activation underlying pigmentary side effects).

The mechanism is CNS-mediated rather than vascular. Agonism of hypothalamic melanocortin receptors in the paraventricular nucleus and medial preoptic area modulates the dopaminergic sexual-arousal pathway, increasing dopamine release in brain regions mapped to sexual desire. This is why bremelanotide can influence the 'desire' side of sexual response independently of the peripheral, blood-flow mechanism targeted by PDE5 inhibitors. A notable pharmacokinetic feature is that behavioral effects on sexual function persist for roughly 4-8 hours despite a plasma half-life of only about 2.7 hours.
benefits: FDA-approved (as Vyleesi) for acquired, generalized HSDD in premenopausal women
Centrally acting: influences sexual desire/arousal, not just peripheral blood flow
On-demand ('as needed') use rather than daily continuous dosing
Studied off-label in men for erectile function and low libido
Works through a mechanism distinct from PDE5 inhibitors, so it does not rely on vasodilation
risks: Approved only for a narrow indication (premenopausal HSDD); use in men or for general 'libido enhancement' is off-label and largely unstudied
Compounded/research-grade PT-141 is not FDA-approved and lacks purity/sterility guarantees
Transient increases in blood pressure and decreases in heart rate after each dose; caution with uncontrolled hypertension or cardiovascular disease
Contraindicated in people with uncontrolled hypertension or known cardiovascular disease per the Vyleesi label
Focal hyperpigmentation (skin/gum darkening) can develop, especially with frequent dosing and in darker skin, and may not fully reverse
Not established as safe in pregnancy or breastfeeding
sideEffects: Nausea (very common; reported by ~40% in trials, sometimes requiring anti-emetics)
Transient blood pressure elevation and heart-rate reduction (usually resolve within ~12 hours)
Focal hyperpigmentation of face, gums, or breasts with repeated/frequent use
Vomiting, dizziness, or fatigue in some users

dosage.notes: The FDA-approved Vyleesi dose is 1.75 mg subcutaneously (abdomen or thigh) as needed, at least 45 minutes before anticipated sexual activity. Research/community protocols often start lower (~1 mg) to gauge nausea and blood-pressure response before moving to the standard dose. This is a common educational protocol, not medical advice.
dosage.timing: Dosed 'as needed' (PRN), roughly 45 minutes to a few hours before anticipated activity, rather than on a fixed daily schedule. Effects on desire/arousal can persist several hours after a single dose.
dosage.maxDose: Do not take more than one dose within any 24-hour period, and no more than 8 doses per month. Dosing more frequently (e.g., daily) sharply increases the risk of focal hyperpigmentation and additive blood-pressure effects.

steps.note: A lower initial dose is commonly used to assess nausea and blood-pressure response before moving to the standard dose.
1.75 mg subcutaneously as needed, at least 45 minutes before activity. Max 1 dose/24h and 8 doses/month.

frequency: as needed (PRN); no more than once per 24 hours and up to 8 doses per month
halfLife: Plasma half-life ~2.7 hours; behavioral effects on sexual function can persist ~4-8 hours
cycleLength: Not cycled continuously; used on-demand per intimate occasion rather than in fixed multi-week cycles

reconstitution.notes: 10 mg vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 5 mg/mL (5000 mcg/mL), so a 1.75 mg dose = 0.35 mL (35 units on a U-100 insulin syringe) and 1 mg = 0.2 mL (20 units). The approved Vyleesi product ships as a pre-filled 1.75 mg/0.3 mL autoinjector and does not require reconstitution.

storage: Store lyophilized vials refrigerated or frozen and protected from light. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within ~4 weeks. The pre-filled Vyleesi autoinjector is stored at room temperature per its label.
contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension or known cardiovascular disease (per Vyleesi label)
History of significant focal hyperpigmentation or darker skin types prone to it (use with caution / less frequent dosing)
Known hypersensitivity to bremelanotide or melanocortin agonists

interactions.note: Both are melanocortin receptor agonists (PT-141 is derived from the Melanotan-II lineage). Combining or overlapping them risks additive melanocortin effects: nausea, blood-pressure changes, and especially focal hyperpigmentation. Avoid stacking; do not layer PT-141 on top of ongoing Melanotan II dosing.

references.title: VYLEESI (bremelanotide injection) - FDA Full Prescribing Information (accessdata.fda.gov)
Bremelanotide for the treatment of hypoactive sexual desire disorder (PubMed)
Bremelanotide for Female Sexual Dysfunctions: A Review (PMC)
